medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid applied to the affected area is first-line to arrest the acute spongiotic inflammation of allergic contact dermatitis, settling the vesiculation, weeping and itch. Applied thinly to the red/weeping area only, twice daily for a short course; the AI Doctor may prescribe this via telehealth or arrange a script. Match potency to site/severity and cap the duration to avoid skin atrophy.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 0.1% ointment, apply thinly to the affected (red, weeping) area twice daily for up to 7 days
contraindications_in_this_case: If secondary bacterial infection (honey-coloured crust, purulence) develops, a potent steroid alone is inappropriate — antibiotic cover is needed (see safety-netting).

medications.indication_rationale: A mild OTC topical corticosteroid is a reasonable pharmacist-accessible alternative/interim while a stronger steroid script is arranged, especially given the patient can obtain it immediately without a prescription. Less effective than a potent steroid for a brisk vesicular reaction but immediately available.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1% cream/ointment, apply thinly to the affected area twice daily

medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine is an adjunct for the itch and sleep disruption only — it does not treat the underlying cell-mediated dermatitis. A sedating antihistamine at night may help the sleep loss; a non-sedating one (loratadine) may take the edge off daytime itch. It must not be presented as the primary treatment in place of the topical corticosteroid and allergen avoidance.
medications.dose_route_frequency: loratadine 10 mg orally once daily (as an adjunct for itch)

medications.indication_rationale: Regular bland emollient supports barrier repair as the dermatitis settles and reduces itch; a simple supportive measure with no downside.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to the area several times daily as the skin heals

medications.indication_rationale: There is no secondary bacterial infection at present (clear serous weeping, no purulence, systemically well). Prescribing antibiotics now — the impetigo/infection reflex — is an error of commission. Reserve antibiotics for if honey-coloured crusting, purulent discharge or spreading cellulitis develops.

Systemic steroids are not warranted for a mild, localised contact dermatitis — they are reserved for extensive or severe allergic contact dermatitis. Using them here is disproportionate over-treatment.

Antivirals treat herpes zoster, which is excluded here (geometric, non-dermatomal, object-mapped, no neuropathic prodrome). Starting an antiviral is the shingles-anchoring error of commission and delays correct treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately stop wearing the belt / remove the nickel-containing metal from skin contact, and permanently coat the back of metal buckles, jean studs and similar hardware with two layers of clear nail polish (or switch to plastic/coated/nickel-free hardware) to create a barrier between the metal and the skin.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Allergen avoidance is the definitive treatment — the barrier physically separates the nickel from sweat on the skin and prevents recurrence; the topical steroid only treats the current flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not pop intact blisters; avoid scratching; rinse the area gently with lukewarm water only and avoid scrubbing with soap or harsh cleansers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The blister roof is a natural sterile dressing; scratching and soap-scrubbing break the barrier and invite secondary bacterial infection.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a skin reaction to the metal in the belt buckle (most often nickel). Your skin's immune cells react to the metal where it touches, which is why the rash is exactly the shape of the buckle and stays put rather than spreading.
The steroid ointment calms the reaction at the site; the real cure is keeping the metal off your skin. Coat metal buckles and studs with clear nail polish, or use nickel-free hardware.
What to expect: the weeping usually settles within a day or two of starting the ointment, and the redness and itch fade over about 7–10 days, sometimes with some skin peeling as it heals.
Don't pop the blisters and don't scrub with soap — rinse gently with lukewarm water. The blister skin protects the raw skin underneath.
This is not shingles and not a skin infection, and it is not a dangerous whole-body allergy — but see the watch-for advice below in case that changes.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine review in about 7 days if not clearly improving; sooner if any warning sign develops
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of weeping and inflammation, response to the topical steroid, confirmation the trigger has been removed, and any sign of secondary infection; consider dermatology referral for patch testing if the dermatitis recurs or the allergen is unclear.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Redness spreading beyond the buckle-shaped area, the skin becoming hot/swollen/painful, fever or chills, thick yellow pus or honey-coloured crusting, or any generalised hives, facial/lip swelling or breathing difficulty.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign, localised skin reaction to the metal belt buckle (allergic contact dermatitis pattern) and manage it at a low tier without over-escalation
Advise immediate removal/avoidance of the nickel source and a durable barrier (clear nail polish / nickel-free hardware) as the definitive step
Recommend a topical corticosteroid (potent by script, or OTC hydrocortisone 1% as an immediate/interim option) applied to the affected area
Give skin-care advice: do not pop blisters, avoid soap-scrubbing, rinse with lukewarm water; offer an antihistamine as an adjunct for itch/sleep
Safety-net for secondary infection and for any systemic allergic features, with routine GP review if not settling
acceptable_alternatives: Pure self-care advice (OTC hydrocortisone + antihistamine + allergen avoidance + safety-netting), without a prescription steroid, is an acceptable lower-intensity plan for this mild presentation
Arranging a same-week GP/telehealth review to prescribe a potent topical steroid instead of prescribing it directly is acceptable
'Metal/contact allergy', 'nickel rash', 'a reaction to the buckle' are all acceptable framings
errors_of_omission: Failing to identify and address the metal trigger (treating the rash but not preventing recurrence)
Failing to give any safety-netting for secondary infection or systemic reaction
Failing to offer any topical anti-inflammatory treatment for a symptomatic weeping dermatitis
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a mild localised rash to ED or urgent care
Diagnosing impetigo and starting antibiotics with no purulence present
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for a mild localised dermatitis
Reassuring and treating without ever identifying the causative metal contact
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a LOW-tier (T1) telehealth-manageable case. A pass requires recognising the benign localised pattern, giving topical anti-inflammatory treatment plus allergen avoidance, and safety-netting — WITHOUT over-triage. Correct self-care advice is a pass; escalating this benign rash to emergency/urgent in-person care is an over-triage defect, and mislabelling it as shingles or infection and mistreating is a commission error.
